name: Vitamin D supplementation for preeclampsia
comparator: Placebo or low-dose vitamin D (subgroup separated)
summary: Across 14 randomised trials of preeclampsia and 13 of preterm labor, prenatal vitamin D supplementation was associated with a significant reduction in each (pooled RR ~0.55 and ~0.70 in the source review). Effects on low birth weight and 5-minute Apgar did not reach significance. Benefit was more pronounced against placebo than against low-dose vitamin D controls.
description: A meta-analysis (Moghib 2024, BMC Pregnancy and Childbirth) of 33 RCTs of prenatal vitamin D supplementation. Each outcome's constituent trials are entered from the review's forest plots (Figures 3–7) — raw per-arm event counts for the binary outcomes, per-arm mean±SD for the continuous 5-minute Apgar and 25-hydroxyvitamin D outcomes — and pooled live. Not every trial contributed to every outcome; only trials the review actually plotted for a given outcome are encoded there.
interpretation: The review's pooled effect for preeclampsia is large (RR 0.55), but that number rests on 14 trials of heterogeneous design (open-label to double-blind), heterogeneous doses (400 IU/day to a single 200,000 IU dose), and heterogeneous populations (general pregnancy to women with prior preeclampsia). The authors noted significant publication-bias signals. Subgroup analysis (baked into the encoded trials via the 'vs placebo' / 'vs low-dose vitamin D' subgroup notes) mattered: benefit was much clearer versus placebo than versus a low-dose active control, which is consistent with a threshold effect at correcting deficiency rather than a dose-response of supplementation itself. The preterm-labor result is dominated by Roth 2018 (Bangladesh, 1300 women), the largest trial, which found the opposite direction (RR ~1.5) — the pooled reduction depends heavily on the smaller, deficiency-focused trials. Low birth weight and 5-minute Apgar did not reach significance. As with any single-review estimate: absolute benefit depends on baseline preeclampsia risk in the population, and the ~45% relative reduction here should not be assumed to travel to well-nourished, vitamin-D-replete populations.
methodologyNotes: All outcomes are computed live from each trial's raw data (2×2 counts or per-arm mean±SD) — nothing hardcoded. The review's pooled random-effects estimates reproduce here within rounding (preeclampsia ≈0.55, preterm labor ≈0.70, low birth weight ≈0.65, Apgar MD ≈0.20, 25-OH MD ≈32.4 nmol/L). Trials are entered only for the outcomes the review actually plotted for them (Figures 3–7). The 25-hydroxyvitamin D outcome (Figure 5) is a biomarker confirming the supplement worked, not a clinical outcome; it is included so that every trial the review pooled is represented. Multi-arm trials that the review split across a shared control arm are entered exactly as the review plotted them: Rostami 2018 contributes two rows to preeclampsia and preterm labor (moderate/severe-deficiency strata, * and **); Grant 2014 and Mallet 1986 each contribute two dose arms, and Roth 2018 four dose arms, to the 25-OH forest (each against the shared placebo). These are the review's own rows, not double-counted trials, and each trial is still counted once in the topic totals (by citation). The subgroup structure (intervention vs placebo vs low-dose vitamin D) is captured in each study's `notes`. Of the review's 33 included RCTs, 29 appear in these forest plots and are encoded here; four (Kaur 1991, Shahgheibi 2016, Sunarno 2023, Yu 2009) reported only endpoints the review did not pool into any of these forests (birth/placental weight, gestational diabetes, blood pressure, or non-poolable vitamin D status) and so have no per-arm data to compute — they are named here for completeness. The review reported significant publication bias (Egger test).

outcomes.label: Preeclampsia (incidence)
outcomes.description: Incident preeclampsia during pregnancy (per each trial's diagnostic criteria).

outcomes.label: Low birth weight
outcomes.description: Birth weight below the trial's low-birth-weight threshold (typically <2500 g).

outcomes.label: Apgar score at 5 minutes
outcomes.description: Apgar score at 5 minutes (higher is better).

outcomes.label: Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D (nmol/L)
outcomes.description: Maternal serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D level (nmol/L). A biomarker confirming the supplement raised vitamin D status, not a clinical outcome.
